title: The Fourth Sunday of Easter
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
## Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
## May 3, 2020 | Preacher: Richard Lawson

---

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This is the Fourth Sunday of Easter, also called Good Shepherd Sunday, falling within the Easter Season (Eastertide). The RCL readings for this date would include Acts 4:32-35, Psalm 23, 1 John 1:1-2:2, and John 10:11-18, with the Gospel centering on Jesus as the Good Shepherd. This Sunday sits squarely in the season of Resurrection joy, between Easter (April 12, 2020) and Pentecost (May 31, 2020).

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was navigating the early institutional responses to COVID-19 closures and remote worship, with most dioceses still under stay-at-home orders and experimenting with virtual services. The Diocese of Colorado had suspended in-person worship since mid-March. No major denominational news (General Convention elections, major schisms) occurred this specific week, but the broader context was one of unprecedented disruption to parish life and sacramental practice across the communion.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was in the fifth week of Colorado's stay-at-home order (issued March 25), with restrictions beginning slight relaxation phases but parks and most businesses still closed. The weather in early May was typical spring (mild, 60s-70s). Local news focused on frontline workers and economic crisis impacts on Colorado's hospitality and outdoor recreation industries. The Capitol Hill area, home to Saint John's Cathedral, was quiet with minimal foot traffic and most neighborhood businesses shuttered or operating at reduced capacity.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The US had surpassed 1 million confirmed COVID-19 cases by May 1, with over 67,000 deaths nationally. President Trump was promoting hydroxychloroquine and suggesting injections of disinfectant during briefings (April 23), drawing widespread criticism from medical professionals. The economic collapse was accelerating, with unemployment claims exceeding 30 million since mid-March. The national debate centered on when/how to reopen the economy versus maintaining lockdown measures.

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

India was entering a severe surge in cases, with lockdown beginning to strain its healthcare system. The UK death toll from COVID-19 had surpassed Italy's, making it Europe's hardest-hit nation. Brazil's President Bolsonaro continued downplaying the pandemic while cases multiplied, creating deep internal political tension and international criticism.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

The US was experiencing acute collective anxiety and grief with pervasive isolation, economic dread, and medical uncertainty defining daily life. Entertainment was largely confined to streaming and home-based activities; sports remained suspended (no NBA, MLB, or live entertainment). Churches nationwide were wrestling with profound theological and pastoral questions about suffering, divine care, and community in isolation—making the Good Shepherd text particularly resonant. There was a surreal quality to American life: simultaneous recognition of mass death and nationwide shutdown alongside early-May spring weather and political conflict over restrictions.
